coredata.dc:title: Fluorinated carbon nanodot-line/microfiber coupling textile with long-lived water-repellency for reliably monitoring human motion under amphibious environment
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Junchi
Li, Zhaohang
Song, Haoyang
Xu, Xinyu
Long, Cai
Li, Yunrui
Qing, Yongquan
Liu, Changsheng
coredata.dc:description: Superhydrophobic and conductive textile-based composites exhibit significant potential for detecting human behavior in amphibious environment. However, the achievement of superhydrophobicity by the incorporation of hydrophobic insulating micro-nanoparticles often impairs their electrical conductivity and strain-sensing capabilities, hindering practical applications. Herein, we developed a long-lived fluorinated carbon nanodot-line/microfiber coupling textile (FCNCT) that is simultaneously excellent stretchability, water-repellency, and conductivity. Such textiles utilize the synergistic effects of the zero-dimensional carbon black, one-dimensional carbon nanotubes, and polyvinylidene fluoride components, ensuring superior sensitive strain detection while maintaining a robust superhydrophobic surface. Importantly, the FCNCT exhibits remarkable strain-sensing properties, including a wide linear sensing range (strain ∼ 120%), high sensing sensitivity (gauge factor ∼ 9.4), low detection limit (strain ∼ 0.5%), and fast response/recovery time (100/150 ms). These features enable FCNCT to reliably monitor full-range human behaviors across various conditions-dry, wet, and underwater. This textile-based composite holds great promise in advancing the development of wearable electronic textiles in amphibious environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Superhydrophobic
Textile-based composite
Synergistic effects
Full-range human behaviors
Amphibious environment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design concept for achieving the FCNCT
Wearable sensing application under amphibious environments

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2302012) and the Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2023-MS-079).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of the FCNCT. (a) Schematic illustration of fabricating FCNCT. (b) Mechanism description of conductivity enhancement based on the coupled fluorinated carbon nanodot-line. (c) The surface resistance of the FCNCT. (d) The low adhesion behavior between water droplet and FCNCT surface. (e) The phenomenon of continuous water droplets bouncing off the surface FCNCT. (f) Anti-wetting properties of FCNCT under stretching, bending, and twisting states. (g) Optical images of FCNCT with anti-wettability for different droplets.

Structural characterization of the FCNCT. (a,b) The SEM images of the nylon textile. (c,d) The SEM and (e) EDS mapping images of the FCNCT. The 3D morphology images of (f) the nylon textile and (g) the FCNCT. (h) The SEM image of the FC-nanoline composite coating. (i) The SEM image of the FC-nanodot composite coating.

Electromechanical properties of the FCNCT strain sensor. (a) Relative resistance changes of the textile coated with FC-nanodot composite, FC-nanoline composite, and FC-nanodot-line composite, towards external strain. (b) The strain sensing mechanism schematic diagram of these coatings. (c-e) The response/recovery time of the FCNCT sensor under the low limit of strain detection (0.5 % strain). (f) Relative resistance changes of the FCNCT sensor under different strain states. Strain sensing behavior of the FCNCT sensor under repetitive stretching/releasing (g) at various maximum strains (10 %, 20 %, 40 %, 80 % and 100 %) and (h) at maximum strain of 50 % with different frequencies (0.2 Hz, 0.5 Hz, 1 Hz, and 2 Hz). (i) Long-term strain sensing performance of the FCNCT sensor under 1500 cyclic stretching and releasing (50 % strain).

The superhydrophobic property of FCNCT surface under dynamic tensile state. (a) The shape of the water droplet standing on the FCNCT surface corresponding to different stretching states. (b) The WCAs on the FCNCT surface with different strains from 0% to 200%. (c) Optical pictures of different droplets on the surface of FCNCT under 200% strain. (d) Direct antifouling performance of the FCNCT under 200% strain. (e) Indirect antifouling performance of the FCNCT under 200% strain.

Robustness of FCNCT surface. (a) The WCA and resistance of FCNCT surface after the 1000 stretching-releasing cycles (20 % strain). The WCA and resistance of FCNCT surface after the 1000 (a) stretching-releasing, (b) bending-releasing and (c) twisting-releasing cycles, respectively. (d) The flexibility test of FCNCT that was pinched by hand for 3 times. (e) Superhydrophobic photographs of FCNCT surface after knife scraping test. (f) The WCA and resistance of FCNCT surface after 40 times tape peeling. (g) The WCA and SA of FCNCT surface after 20 m sandpaper abrasion. (h) Superhydrophobicity of the FCNCT surface after water impact for 1 min.

The human behaviors detection of FCNCT sensor under amphibious environments, including dry, rainy, and underwater conditions. Real-time human large deformation behaviors detection of FCNCT sensor, including (a,b) finger, (c,d) wrist, (e) elbow, and (f,g) knee. Real-time human subtle deformation behaviors detection of FCNCT sensor, including (h) upper arm and (i) forearm muscles. Real-time human physiological signs detection of FCNCT sensor, including (j) breathing and (k) speaking.

The influence of water on the FCNCT sensor servicing in water. (a) Optical images and (b) sensing signals of the FCNCT sensor monitoring finger bending with an angle of 90° in water. (c) Optical images of the FCNCT sensor before and after bending 100 cycles (90°) in water. (d) Schematic diagram of different depths of sensor in water. (e) Resistance response of FCNCT sensor when it was dipped into the water. (f) Resistance response of FCNCT sensor when it was repeatedly dipped into the water.

head.ce:title: Fluorinated carbon nanodot-line/microfiber coupling textile with long-lived water-repellency for reliably monitoring human motion under amphibious environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Anisotropy and Texture of Materials, Ministry of Education, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Anisotropy and Texture of Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Anisotropy and Texture of Materials, Ministry of Education, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang 110016, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang 110016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Province Shiyan High School, Shenyang 110841, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Province Shiyan High School, Shenyang 110841, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Superhydrophobic and conductive textile-based composites exhibit significant potential for detecting human behavior in amphibious environment. However, the achievement of superhydrophobicity by the incorporation of hydrophobic insulating micro-nanoparticles often impairs their electrical conductivity and strain-sensing capabilities, hindering practical applications. Herein, we developed a long-lived fluorinated carbon nanodot-line/microfiber coupling textile (FCNCT) that is simultaneously excellent stretchability, water-repellency, and conductivity. Such textiles utilize the synergistic effects of the zero-dimensional carbon black, one-dimensional carbon nanotubes, and polyvinylidene fluoride components, ensuring superior sensitive strain detection while maintaining a robust superhydrophobic surface. Importantly, the FCNCT exhibits remarkable strain-sensing properties, including a wide linear sensing range (strain ∼ 120%), high sensing sensitivity (gauge factor ∼ 9.4), low detection limit (strain ∼ 0.5%), and fast response/recovery time (100/150 ms). These features enable FCNCT to reliably monitor full-range human behaviors across various conditions-dry, wet, and underwater. This textile-based composite holds great promise in advancing the development of wearable electronic textiles in amphibious environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Textile-based composites are currently a focus of research in the wearable electronics field and play an important role in human motion detection, personal health monitoring, and human–machine interactions [1–3]. In general, textile-based resistance strain sensors, which can transduce mechanical deformation into electrical signals, are produced by integrating stretchable, flexible, breathable, and comfortable ordinary textiles (substrate materials) with conductive functional materials (strain sensing materials) [4–8]. Strain sensing materials can form conductive networks on the surface of textiles that can produce recoverable changes under tensile stress. It’s well-known that the sensing performance, including the sensing sensitivity, sensing range, response/recovery time, low limit of detection, and durability, mainly depends on the composition and structure of the conductive material, and the integration between interfaces [9,10].

Currently, carbonaceous conductive nanomaterials (e.g., carbon black, carbon nanotubes, graphene, and MXene) are commonly used in the fabrication of textile-based strain sensors with high sensing performance due to their excellent conductivity and controllable micro/nanostructures [11–14]. For instance, Zhang et al. [15] fabricated a wearable strain sensor based on cotton fabric coated with single-walled carbon nanotubes, which showed a maximum linear sensing strain of 20 %, and sensitivity with a gauge factor (GF) of 1.5. Li et al. [16] developed a nonwoven fabric-based flexible sensor with a special microcrack structure, it was dip-coated by conductive MXene/cellulose nanocrystals and displayed a wide sensing range of 83 % and high sensitivity (GF of 3405), but its linear sensing strain was only 20 % (GF ∼ 50). Qu et al. [17] prepared a smart textile-based strain sensor that exhibited a GF of 2.6 with a linear response range up to 120 %, it was fabricated by decorating core-spun yarn with carbon black (CB) nanoparticles and polydopamine (PDA). Therefore, it is still an enormous challenge to prepare a textile-based strain sensor which can achieve high sensitivity (> 10) and broad sensing range (> 50 %) with high linearity (> 99 %), simultaneously [18,19]. In addition, another problem of textile-based strain sensors comes from their own hydrophilicity; they will inevitably face humid and corrosive conditions in practical applications, which could be produced by the human body or the external environment, such as sweat, rain, and fog vapor [20–22]. In this case, the conductivity-deformation characteristics of the strain sensing part will be interfered and even damaged in the form of detachment decomposition, or oxidation, resulting in reduced service life [23,24].

Inspired by the natural phenomenon that water droplets easily roll off the surfaces of lotus leaves and peanut leaves [25–29], superhydrophobic surface with large water contact angle > 150° and small sliding angle < 10° has been successfully applied in many fields, such as self-cleaning [30], anticorrosion [31], anti-icing [32–34], oil–water separation [35–37] and wearable sensors [38]. Therefore, the combination of superhydrophobic functional surface and textile-based strain sensor is proposed to solve the influence of harsh practical conditions, including high water content or even underwater environments [39–43]. And the contaminants (e.g., dirt, grit, and oil) adhered to the surface of superhydrophobic sensors can be easily cleaned, further enhancing their sensing stability in daily life [44–46]. The rough hierarchal micro-nano structure and low surface energy are essential to introduce superhydrophobicity into textile-based sensors, but the particles (e.g., TiO2, SiO2, and MnO2) used to improve the rough structure and substance with low surface energy will degrade its strain sensing performance [47–49]. For example, the superhydrophobic/conductive nanofiber composite strain sensor based on a hierarchical SiO2/graphene micro-nanostructure only achieved a narrow linear sensing range of 20 % strain with a GF of 1.1 [50]. Furthermore, Peng et al. [51] successfully fabricated a superhydrophobic fabric strain sensor by coating PDA, multi-walled carbon nanotubes, silver nanoparticles, and polydimethylsiloxane onto nonwoven fabrics step-by-step. Although the fabric-based sensor exhibited a high GF of 27.5, its maximum linear sensing strain was less than 20 %. Although these textile-based sensors have achieved superhydrophobic surface, the strain sensing performance of these sensors are greatly reduced. Therefore, it is urgently desirable to enhance the strain sensing performance while realizing the waterproof property of textile-based strain sensors.

In this work, we fabricated a long-lived fluorinated carbon nanodot-line/microfiber coupling textile (FCNCT) via the dip-coating of nylon textiles with a mixture of zero-dimensional carbon black, one-dimensional carbon nanotubes, and polyvinylidene fluoride hybrids. This unique blend tapped into the synergistic effects of the various components not only constructed more conductive pathways in the coating, enhancing the conductivity of the FCNCT, but also provided rougher micro-nano structure for achieving a superhydrophobic surface of FCNCT, further obtaining excellent antifouling and self-cleaning ability. The excellent interfacial bonding between polyvinylidene fluoride and nylon textile fibers also endowed FCNCT with satisfactory mechanical and chemical robustness. In addition, the fabricated FCNCT strain sensor exhibited a wide linear sensing range of 120 % strain, high GF up to 9.4, response/recovery time of 100/150 ms, and low detection limit of 0.5 % strain, achieving full-range human behavior detection capability under various conditions-dry, wet, and underwater. Furthermore, it is pointed out that the different performance of sensors in air and underwater environment may be attributed to water pressure.

Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (carbon nanoline) and carbon black (carbon nanodot) were purchased from Jiangsu Xianfeng Nano Material Co., Ltd. 1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorodecyltriethoxysilane (FS) was procured from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Co., Ltd. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) with molecular weight of 1.2 million, was supplied by Dongguan Zhanyang Teflon Co., Ltd. N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC) was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory Co., Ltd. Nylon textiles (93.1 % nylon and 6.9 % spandex) were obtained from Xinji Damai Clothing Co., Ltd. Heat-cured conductive silver paste was supplied by Shenzhen Woweisi Electronic Technology Co., Ltd. The waterproof polyurethane (PU) film was purchased from Jiangsu Guangyi Medical Dressing Co., Ltd.

Carbon nanodot (0.01 g) and carbon nanoline (0.09 g) were first added in the anhydrous ethanol (40 mL) and the contents were ultrasound for 10 min. Then added FS (150 µL) into the solution and magnetically stirred for 3 h at 60 °C (pH = 5). After drying the reacted mixture in an oven (100 °C), the fluorinated carbon nanodot-line (FC-nanodot-line) powder was obtained. The PVDF (0.01 g) was added into the DMAC (40 mL) and stirred at 80 °C until the PVDF dissolved completely in the solvent. And then the as-prepared FC-nanodot-line powder was added into it and treated by ultrasound for 0.5 h to yield the conductive suspension.

Finally, the nylon textile was immersed into the FC-nanodot-line composite suspension for 30 min followed by drying at 80 °C for 45 min, the FCNCT was obtained. Before the dip-coating process, the textile was washing by ethanol to remove the impurities on the fiber surface.

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS10, Thermo, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher, USA) were employed to analyze the chemical structure of the samples. The laser scanning confocal microscope (LEXT OLS4100, Olympus Corporation, Japan) was used to investigate the surface roughness and 3D morphology of the textiles. These surface morphologies were further obtained using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Apreo 2C, Thermo Fisher, USA). The water contact angles (WCAs) and sliding angles (SAs) were examined by an optical CA meter device (JCY-2, Fangrui, China). These results were measured from at least five different locations of each sample. The FCNCT was cut into the uniform size of 4 cm × 1 cm, and both ends of the textile were related to two copper wires by conductive silver paste. Then the electrodes were encapsulated with waterproof tape to prevent the damage of water. This assembled FCNCT strain sensor was used in the strain sensing test and human motion detection. The mechanical–electrical performance of the textile sensor was tested by a universal testing machine (XM-DZSC001, Yitong Testing Equipment Technology Co., Ltd.) and simultaneously recording the real-time electrical resistance using digital source meter (2638A, FLUKE, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design concept for achieving the FCNCT
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve a waterproof and conductive textile that can monitor human motion under amphibious environment, we design the FCNCT (composed of nylon textile and conductive coating) based on the following two criteria: (i) The coating must construct excellent conductive network and endow the textile with superhydrophobic surface. For this aim, zero-dimensional carbon black (carbon nanodot) with diameter of ∼25 nm (Fig. S1, Supporting Information) and one-dimensional carbon nanotubes (carbon nanoline) with outer diameter of ∼20 nm, inner diameter of ∼10 nm, and length of ∼15 µm (Fig. S1, Supporting Information) were selected to be hybridized as dispersed phase in the coating, because they could simultaneously provide rough structure for superhydrophobic surface (Fig. 1 a) and more conductive pathways for conductivity (Fig. 1b). In addition, the hydrolysis of FS results in the conversion of siloxane group Si–OC2H5 to silanol group Si–OH and then reacted with the hydroxyl groups on the surface of carbon nanodot and carbon nanoline particles, providing –CF2– and –CF3 groups (low-surface-energy groups) to reduce the surface energy for achieving superhydrophobic surface (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). From the FTIR spectra of fluorinated carbon nanodot-line (Fig. S4a, Supporting Information), the newly formed peaks at 1150 and 1207 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of C–F bonds of –CF2– and –CF3 functional groups from FS, and a new absorption peak at 970 cm−1 was attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of Si–O–Si bonds [52–54]. The result proved that FS with low surface energy was successfully grafted onto the surfaces of carbon nanodot and carbon nanoline. (ii) There must be excellent adhesion strength between coating and fiber interfaces. Therefore, the PVDF was used as continuous phase to solve the problem of poor bonding between fluorinated carbon nanodot-line and fibers of nylon textile, enhancing the robustness of the coating during application. On the one hand, the dispersed phase (FC-nanodot-line) could exhibit good dispersion in the PVDF, resulting in excellent conductivity [55,56]. On the other hand, the PVDF also riches in –CF2– groups with low surface energy, which is one of the important composition premises for constructing superhydrophobic surface [57,58]. The XPS results further demonstrated that the FC-nanodot-line composite coating with these low surface energy groups were immobilized on the surface of the textile fiber (Fig. S4b-d, Supporting Information).

As we expected, the prepared FCNCT (10 × 40 mm2) achieved excellent conductivity with surface resistance of 7.8 kΩ (sheet resistance of ∼2.6 × 104 kΩ/m2) (Fig. 1c), and superhydrophobicity (WCA of 159.1°) which could maintain even under the state of common mechanical deformation (e.g., stretching, bending, and twisting) (Fig. 1f). In contrast, the carbon nanodot-line/microfiber coupling textile (CNCT) exhibited hydrophilicity and was easily wetted by water droplets (Fig. S5, Supporting Information). The FCNCT also exhibited strong adhesion resistance to water droplets, attributed to the air layer formed between its surface and the water droplet (Fig. 1d). Moreover, the water sprayed onto its surface bounced back quickly (Fig. 1e), which also proved the excellent waterproof performance of the FCNCT (Video S1, Supporting Information). As shown in Fig. 1g, the FCNCT also exhibited non-wettability to common daily liquid droplets (e.g., NaCl aq, tea, milk, cola, coffee, and juice) and even to acid (pH = 1) and alkaline (pH = 13) solutions, demonstrating excellent liquid repellency and corrosion resistance. In addition, the superhydrophobic surface endowed the FCNCT outstanding direct and indirect antifouling abilities attributed to the low sliding angle (6.1°). The various liquids commonly seen in daily life (e.g., water, NaCl aq, milk, tea, coffee, cola, juice, pH = 1, and pH = 13) could roll down its surface (tilted by 10°) freely and quickly (Fig. S6a and Video S2, Supporting Information), the phenomenon proved the direct antifouling property of the FCNCT. The water droplets could further effectively remove the soil particles presented in the droplet’s rolling path off the FCNCT surface, leaving a clean surface without any signs of wetting or residue (Fig. S6b and Video S3, Supporting Information) attributed to its excellent self-cleaning ability. In contrast, the textile coated with FC-nanoline composite was easily wetted by the water resulting in the corrosion damage to the coating (Fig. S6c, Supporting Information). And the worse damage was produced, when the textile was covered with soil particles (Fig. S6d, Supporting Information).

The SEM and 3D morphology images of the original nylon textile and FCNCT surfaces are shown in Fig. 2 a–g. The fiber surface of the original textile showed a smooth and neat appearance (Fig. 2a,b), endowed the textile with a surface roughness of 21.6 µm (Fig. 2f). The original textile exhibited superhydrophilicity. After coated FC-nanodot-line composite, the FCNCT surface obtained rough hierarchical structure (Fig. 2c) with a roughness of 60.6 µm (Fig. 2g) formed by the continuous accumulation of FC-nanodot-line and coupling with microfiber (Fig. 2d). The EDS mapping further confirmed that the fiber of FCNCT has been homogenously covered with the functional nanocomposite (Fig. 2e). Therefore, the sufficiently rough hierarchical structure of the FCNCT contributed to the achievement of the superhydrophobic surface. In addition, it is observed that there were three types of dispersed phase/dispersed phase contact in the fluorinated carbon nanodot-line composite coating, i.e., fluorinated nanodot/dot, nanodot-line, and nanoline/line contacts, as illustrated in Fig. 2d. In contrast, there were only fluorinated nanoline/line and fluorinated nanodot/dot types in FC-nanoline composite coating (Fig. 2h) and FC-nanodot composite coating (Fig. 2i), respectively. Therefore, the FCNCT could obtain excellent electrical conductivity while realizing superhydrophobic surface. It is closely related to the strain sensing performance of FCNCT.

Generally, the sensing performance of strain sensor is evaluated by sensing sensitivity and linear sensing range. The sensitivity can be calculated by gauge factor (GF = (R-R0)/ε), where R denotes the real-time resistance under the tensile strain (ε), R0 is the initial resistance under the relaxed state. The Fig. 3 a showed the relative resistance changes of the textile coated with FC-nanodot composite, FC-nanoline composite, and FC-nanodot-line composite (FCNCT) in the stretching process. The textile coated with FC-nanodot composite achieved a linear sensing range of 40 % strain (linearity of 96.6 %) with a GF of 5.1, and the textile coated with FC-nanoline composite achieved a linear sensing range of 80 % strain (linearity of 99.1 %) with a GF of 5.3. When the FC-nanodot introduced into the FC-nanoline composite coating, it could build up more conductive networks, providing a high conductive basics (low R0) for high sensing sensitivity (Fig. S2, Supporting Information) [55,56,59]. Meanwhile, such a conductive network could be better maintained in the stretching process, attribute to the coupling hybridization of FC-nanodot and FC-nanoline (Fig. 3b) [57,60,61]. Therefore, the FCNCT achieved a wide linear sensing range of 120 % strain (linearity of 99.5 %) with a GF of 9.4, indicating that both sensitivity and linear sensing range of the FCNCT can be well improved after introducing spherical FC-nanodot (Fig. 1a). The wide linear sensing range contributed to the detection of human motion with large deformation. Moreover, the low limit of strain detection and response/recovery time are also important performance indicators of strain sensors. According to Fig. 3c–e, the response/recovery time of the FCNCT sensor was 100/150 ms under the low limit of strain detection of 0.5 %, that ensured the sensor could accurately monitor real-time human behaviors with tiny deformation. As shown in Fig. 3f, the relative resistance changes of the FCNCT sensor exhibited gradient increase and decrease in various static strain of 0 %, 30 %, 60 %, and 100 %. It is also observed that the sensor could output corresponding stable and continuous response (Fig. 3g) when it was applied different levels of multicycle load (strain of 10 %, 20 %, 40 %, 80 %, and 100 %, respectively). These results demonstrated the sensor's stretch/rebound stability, inevitable requirement for its application. The frequency-independent response characteristic of FCNCT sensor could be proved by the stable and periodic electrical signals corresponding to the stretching/releasing cycles with different frequencies (0.2 Hz, 0.5 Hz, 1 Hz, and 2 Hz), as shown in Fig. 3h. To evaluate the long-term repeatability of the FCNCT strain sensor, 1500 times continuous stretching/releasing cycles were applied to the sensor with a strain of 50 %. In Fig. 3i, the peaks and baselines of the curve were almost consistent in every cycle, exhibiting exceptional durability and reliability. It’s attributed to the excellent elasticity of textile and good adhesion strength between coating and fiber interfaces. This excellent strain sensing performance endowed the FCNCT sensor with the application of full-range human behavior monitoring.

For the high-performance superhydrophobic textile-based strain sensor, it is enormous challenge to simultaneously achieve both wide linear sensing range (>50 %) and high sensitivity. The strain sensing performance of recently superhydrophobic textile-based sensors were compared by the GF values and maximum linear sensing strain ranges (Table. S1, Supporting Information) [22,39,40,43,44,50,51,52,62]. For example, Peng et al. [22] fabricated a MXene‐based fabric sensor, it exhibited a GF of 43.2, but narrow linear sensing range of 20 %. Although the Ag-EGaIn-textile sensor [43] and CNT/F-TPU sensor [62] achieved a linear sensing range greater than 50 %, their GF was only 8.3 and 1.6, respectively. The sensitivity is lower than the FCNCT sensor. In this work, the FCNCT sensor exhibited a wide linear sensing range of 120 % strain (linearity of 99.5 %) with a GF of 9.4. Considering the linear sensing range and sensitivity comprehensively, our FCNCT sensor is relatively advanced.

The influence of stretching on the superhydrophobic of the proposed FCNCT must be considered, because it is expected to be used to monitor human behavior that produced large deformation. The Fig. 4 a demonstrated the spherical shape of the water droplet standing on the FCNCT surface stretched to different states (Fig. S8, Supporting Information). The corresponding WCAs of FCNCT surface decreased from 159.1° to 152.0° with the stretching strain from 0 % to 200 % (Fig. 4b), presenting prominent waterproof performance under mechanical deformation. As shown in Fig. 4c, this dynamic anti-wettability was still applicable to other liquids in life (such as NaCl aq, tea, milk, cola, Coffee, juice, pH = 1, and pH = 13). Similarly, the direct/indirect antifouling behaviors under dynamic stretching could be observed in Fig. 4d,e, the water droplets could easily roll off the FCNCT surface (inclination angle < 10°) even under a high elongation of 200 %, and the soil particles could be carried away by the water droplets dripping on the stretched surface (Video S4, Supporting Information). It reduced the contamination of the textile surface and further improved its stability.

These results showed that the FCNCT possessed outstanding superhydrophobic in the stretching process attributed to the Cassie-Baxter state between the liquid droplet and surface that has always been maintained.

The robustness is essential to the practical application of wearable superhydrophobic and conductive textile. Because, it is inevitably subjected to various complicated conditions such as cyclic mechanical deformation, friction, liquids erosion, and so on. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 a–c, the WCAs and resistance of FCNCT surface fluctuated around 159.1° and 7.8 kΩ, respectively, during the various cyclic mechanical deformation tests including 1000 times stretching-releasing (20 % strain), bending-releasing and twisting-releasing cycles, respectively, presenting excellent mechanical robustness.

In addition, the FCNCT was pinched by hand for 3 times and even there were some wrinkles on its surface, the water droplets could still roll off the surface easily (Video S5, Supporting Information). It could be also observed in Fig. 5d that the water droplets stand on its surface with spherical shapes when the FCNCT was placed horizontally, exhibiting waterproof property against with flexibility. The robust superhydrophobic surface could remain even after knife-scratching, as shown in Fig. 5e. The WCA decreased from 159.1° to 153.1° and the surface resistance increased from 7.8 kΩ to 8.5 kΩ, after 40 times tape peeling (Fig. 5f). And the FCNCT loaded a pressure of 20 g, was pulled on 1000 grit sandpaper to evaluate its abrasion resistance (Fig. 5g). After 16 m sandpaper abrasion, the WCA decreased to151.8°, and the CA increased from 6.1° to 9.9°, maintaining superhydrophobicity. After 20 m sandpaper abrasion, the water droplets could maintain spherical shapes on the FCNCT surface with corresponding WCA of 151.0°, but its SA increased to 12° due to a certain degree of damage to textile fibers. The tape peeling and sandpaper abrasion results indicated that the stability of the coating could meet the requirements of practical application. Moreover, the FCNCT surface exhibited excellent waterproof performance after water impact for 1 min, as illustrated in Fig. 5h (Video S6, Supporting Information). For the aging resistance, the WCA and surface resistance of the FCNCT were almost unchanged even after 80 days (Fig. S9, Supporting Information). When soaked in acid (pH = 1) and alkali (pH = 13) solution respectively, the FCNCT showed 2 h and 1.5 h stability of the superhydrophobic surface (Fig. S10, Supporting Information). Furthermore, the FCNCT showed high temperature resistance, the coating decomposed until the temperature reached to 130 ℃, the hydrophobicity decreased. Up to 150 ℃, the material became hydrophilic (Fig. S11, Supporting Information).

The above results demonstrated the outstanding robustness of the FCNCT surface, the reason is mainly outstanding chemical stability of the fluorinated nanodot-line composite coating and the excellent bonding strength between coating and textile fibers. In addition, the elasticity of the coating and the original nylon textile helps resist the damage of the deformation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wearable sensing application under amphibious environments
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent strain sensing and waterproof performance endowed the FCNCT strain sensor with the ability to monitor large deformation behavior (joints bending) and subtle deformation behavior (muscle contraction) under various environments (dry, rainy, and underwater conditions), even some physiological signs (breathing and speaking). Different human behaviors will produce different strains on the skin surface. Therefore, the FCNCT sensor could identify these motions by monitoring the deformation. For instance, the FCNCT sensor was attached to the finger, it exhibited rapid increase of resistance response with finger bending and a maximum ΔR/R0 of ∼0.6 corresponding to the bending angle of 30° (Fig. 6 a). The sensing curves of each bending cycle were almost the same, indicating its excellent reproducibility. In this part, dropping the intermittent water droplets was used to simulate the rainy conditions. As shown in Fig. 6a, the sensing curves hardly changed under rainy conditions, because water droplets immediately rolled off the superhydrophobic surface of FCNCT. However, it showed a lower sensing response when the sensor was used to monitor finger bending with the same angle in water (Fig. 6a,b), this phenomenon could be attributed to the influence of water pressure. Moreover, the property of joints bending sensing under various environments could be also observed in wrist bending (30°) detection (Fig. 6c,d). In Fig. 6e, the FCNCT sensor showed different ΔR/R0 of 0.7, 1.2, and 2.5 corresponding to elbow bending angle of 30°, 60°, and 90°, respectively. The result proved its ability of different angle detection in joints bending. For knee bending behavior that produced greater strain, the sensor could accurately identify knee bending behavior of 90° with a maximum ΔR/R0 of ∼7 (Fig. 6f). In addition, it could also distinguish different knee bending behaviors originated from walking slowly, walking quickly, and running motions (Fig. 6g). The difference is mainly the width of per-cycle signal corresponding to bending frequencies.

When muscles contract, they will produce a subtle deformation on the skin, this can be accurately identified by FCNCT sensor, thanks to its low strain detection limit and fast response. The Fig. 6h,i proved that the sensor achieved the upper arm and forearm muscles contraction monitoring, even under rainy and underwater environments. Moreover, the FCNCT sensor fixed at the chest could output the relative resistance change signals, increasing with breathing in and decreasing with breathing out (Fig. 6j). It showed that the volunteer’s deep breathing needs 6.2 s and 1.2 s for shallow breathing, providing a novel method for monitoring of breathing rate. In particular, it could be used for voice recognition, when the sensor was attached to vocal cords of the volunteer. In Fig. 6k, the number of syllables corresponded to the number of peaks of the output signals, and the peak value represented the sound intensity, proving its broad development prospect in the field of speech recognition.

Comparing the sensing signals of the same behavior in air and water, it is found that the FCNCT sensor output a smaller maximum ΔR/R0 in water. The following tests are aimed to explain this phenomenon. Firstly, the FCNCT sensor was attached onto the finger and bent with an angle of 90° for over 100 cycles in water (Fig. 7 a–c). As shown in Fig. 7a, the superhydrophobic FCNCT presented an interesting air layer in water which protected the textile from being moist. This air layer could maintain even when the finger bent 90°. Furthermore, the sensing curves of real-time underwater finger bending at an angle of 90° (over 100 cycles), changed periodically in Fig. 7b, demonstrating a long-term finger bending sensing in water. And the FCNCT remained dry after bending for 100 cycles in water, further proved that the water could be effectively prevented from penetrating into the FCNCT by the air layer, ensuing the stable human behavior detection in water. These results indicated that the reason for the decrease of underwater human behavior sensing signals was not related to the wetting of FCNCT. Secondly, the FCNCT sensor was dipped into the water (Fig. 7d). As shown in Fig. 7e, its relative resistance change produced a decrease, when the FCNCT entered the water. After being completely immersed in water, the change of signal became smaller. This phenomenon is mainly attributed to the pressure of water, which makes the conductive textile fibers contact more densely, resulting in decrease of resistance. The regular resistance response of FCNCT sensor when it was repeatedly dipped into the water could further prove the influence from the pressure of water (Fig. 7f). It is equivalent to applying extra pressure to the sensor during stretching. Therefore, under the same strain the FCNCT sensor would output a smaller resistance change, compared that in air, resulting a smaller GF. In water, the FCNCT sensor achieved a wide linear sensing range of 120 % strain (linearity of 98.6 %) with a GF of 5.9 (Fig. S12, Supporting Information). Thus, the FCNCT sensor output a smaller maximum ΔR/R0 underwater corresponding to the same human behavior in air. Fortunately, it realized long-lived recognition of full-range human behaviors in water.

In conclusion, a long-lived fluorinated carbon nanodot-line/microfiber coupling textile (FCNCT) was successful fabricated by decorating nylon textiles with a mixture of zero-dimensional carbon black, one-dimensional carbon nanotubes, and polyvinylidene fluoride hybrids. This unique blend tapped into the synergistic effects of the various components enabled the FCNCT to achieve excellent conductivity (surface resistance of 7.8 kΩ) and superhydrophobic surface (WCA of 159.1°) with direct/indirect antifouling abilities. The outstanding interfacial bonding strength between the coating and microfiber ensured the robustness of the superhydrophobic, even undergoing 1000 stretching-releasing, bending-releasing, and twisting-releasing cycles, respectively. The hand pinching, knife scratching, tape peeling, sandpaper abrasion and water impacting tests further indicated the stability of the coating. Moreover, the FCNCT strain sensor obtained outstanding strain sensing performance with wide linear sensing range (strain ∼120 %), high sensing sensitivity (GF ∼9.4), low detection limit (strain ∼0.5 %), fast response/recovery time (100/150 ms) and good durability (∼1500 cycles). Most importantly, the FCNCT sensor could monitor full-range human behaviors under amphibious environments, including dry, rainy, and underwater conditions. Although its performance of human behaviors monitoring in water is slightly different from that in the air due to the pressure of water, the FCNCT sensor could realize a long-term underwater operation. As a promising candidate waterproof and conductive textile-based composite, the FCNCT exhibited extremely promising for applications under amphibious environments.

Junchi Ma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Zhaohang Li: Validation, Investigation. Haoyang Song: Validation, Investigation. Xinyu Xu: Validation, Investigation. Cai Long: Validation, Investigation. Yunrui Li: Investigation. Yongquan Qing: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Changsheng Liu: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2302012) and the Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2023-MS-079).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.150797.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 1
